MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Sep-26-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances
Rebecca Coll, Esq., QUADRA & COLL, LLP 415-426-3502 rcoll@quadracoll.com Attorney for Quadra & Coll, LLP and Weinstein & Numbers, LLP. (via Zoom Conference)
Ethan Jacobs, Esq., Ethan Jacobs Law Corporation (415) 275-0845 ethan@ejacobslaw.com
Counsel for Defendant Abul Shah

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ABUL SHAH's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

Defendant Abul Shah's motion for summary adjudication is denied. Shah's separate statement fails to comply with California Rules of Court, Rule 3.1350(d), (h). It does not separately identify each cause of action and each supporting material fact claimed to be in dispute. This impedes the court's ability to determine if each claim is disposed of in its entirety. Additionally, Shah concedes his request for summary adjudication as to the fifth cause of action is procedurally defective, as it does not dispose of the entire claim. (Reply 9:24-10:1.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Mar-03-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Ethan Jacobs, Esq. (415.275.0845; ethan@ejacobslaw.com) for Defendant.
No other appearances.

Not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT GREEN EARTH TECHNOLOGIES GROUP, LLC'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS is granted.

Defendant alleges this Court lacks personal jurisdiction due to insufficient contacts between Defendant and California to support any basis for jurisdiction. Plaintiffs allege Defendant is subject to specific jurisdiction because Defendant is a successor in interest and alter ego to Bennington Group, LLC, and an alter ego of Defendant Mr. Abul Shah. Plaintiffs also asks the Court for an order to compel the deposition of Mr. Shah to discover information related to the issue of jurisdiction.

Where, as here, a defendant moves to quash service based on the absence of personal jurisdiction, the burden is on the plaintiff to demonstrate by a preponderance of the competent, admissible evidence that all "jurisdictional facts" necessary to support a finding of personal jurisdiction are present. The plaintiff cannot rely on the unverified allegations in its complaint to satisfy this burden. (Minion v. Sup. Ct. (1985) 169 Cal.App.3d 703, 710.) Moreover, the plaintiff has the right to conduct discovery with regard to the issue of jurisdiction to develop the facts necessary to sustain this burden. (Id.) In order to take jurisdictional discovery, "the plaintiff should demonstrate that discovery is likely to lead to the production of evidence of facts establishing jurisdiction." (In re Automobile Antitrust Cases I and II (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 100, 127.) "Any discovery must be limited to the issue of specific personal jurisdiction based on the required minimum contacts relevant to the jurisdictional analysis." (Burdick v. Super. Ct. (2015) 233 Cal.App.4th 8, 30.)

First, Plaintiffs argue Defendant and Bennington Group, LLC, a non-party to the suit, are alter-egos of each other. "It is correct that a motion to quash for lack of personal jurisdiction does not implicate the merits of the complaint, but the plaintiff, in opposing the defendant's motion to quash, must present evidence to justify a finding that the requisite jurisdictional minimum contacts exist." (Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 540.) Given this record, Plaintiffs fail to present sufficient evidence to show specific jurisdiction based on an alter-ego or successor in interest theory. (See Sonora Diamond Corp., 83 Cal.App.4th at 538-539; Ranza v. Nike, Inc. (9th Cir. 2015) 793 F.3d 1059, 1075.)

Second, the Court finds Plaintiffs have not met their burden to conduct further jurisdictional discovery. Plaintiffs allege that Defendant succeeded to Bennington Group's liabilities because both entities are Delaware LLC's, Bennington Group failed, Mr. Shah formed Green Earth, Mr. Shah identified himself as a member and manager of Green Earth and, both businesses offer the same services. To support their alter ego theory, Plaintiffs allege Mr. Shah formed Defendant, Defendant was formed after Bennington Group could no longer do business due to a $10 million judgment against it, Mr. Shah was a member and "principal" of both entities. and both businesses offer the same services. The Court finds that Plaintiffs have not made the necessary showing to take the depositions Mr. Shah because Plaintiffs have not specifically identified what relevant information, they seek that is related to the issue of jurisdiction.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)
